questions.question: What is the primary difference between a stackful coroutine and a stackless coroutine?
options: Stackful coroutines have their own call stack allowing suspension from any call depth; stackless coroutines are transformed into state machines with a heap-allocated frame and can only suspend at explicit await points
Stackful coroutines are faster because they use the CPU stack directly; stackless coroutines are slower because they must allocate heap memory on every suspension
Stackful coroutines can only use a single thread; stackless coroutines can be distributed across multiple threads
Stackful coroutines are used in C++ and Rust; stackless coroutines are used in Go and Python
questions.explanation: Stackful coroutines (goroutines, fibers) maintain their own complete call stack, which means suspension can happen at any point in the call chain. Stackless coroutines (C++20, Rust async) are compiler-transformed into state machines stored in heap frames, restricting suspension to explicit await/yield points in the coroutine body itself.

questions.question: Why does Rust's Pin type exist, and what problem does it solve for async/await?
questions.explanation: Pin prevents a value from being moved in memory after pinning. This is essential because async functions are compiled to state machines that may contain self-referential pointers (e.g., a local reference pointing to another local in the same frame). If the state machine were moved, those self-referential pointers would dangle. Pin guarantees the pointee stays at a stable address, making self-referential async state machines safe.
options: Pin optimizes memory allocation by pinning state machines to cache lines for better locality
Pin prevents a future from being moved in memory, which is necessary because async state machines can contain self-referential pointers that would dangle if the frame were relocated
Pin is a performance hint that tells the compiler to keep a value in registers instead of spilling to the stack
Pin ensures that async tasks run on a specific OS thread to avoid cross-thread synchronization overhead

questions.question: In C++20 coroutines, what is the role of promise_type?
questions.explanation: promise_type is the customization point that defines how a C++20 coroutine behaves. The compiler creates a coroutine frame and constructs the promise object inside it. The promise_type controls: how results are returned (return_value/return_void), how values are yielded (yield_value), what happens on suspension (initial_suspend, final_suspend), and how exceptions are handled (unhandled_exception). It's the bridge between the compiler's state machine and the user-facing coroutine return type.
options: It promises that the coroutine will complete within a guaranteed time bound
It is the customization point that defines coroutine behavior: result return, yield handling, suspension policy, and exception handling
It is a thread-safety guarantee that ensures only one coroutine accesses shared state at a time
It declares which variables inside the coroutine body must be allocated on the heap instead of the stack

questions.question: You need to handle 100,000 concurrent I/O connections. Which concurrency model is most appropriate and why?
questions.explanation: Coroutines (specifically stackless) are the best fit. At 100,000 concurrent connections with I/O-bound work: OS threads would need ~800 GB of stack memory (100k × 8 MB), goroutines would need ~400 MB (100k × 4 KB), and stackless coroutines would need only ~6 MB (100k × 60 bytes). Since I/O-bound work spends most time waiting, the lightweight context-switch cost of coroutines (~10 ns vs ~1–10 μs for threads) makes them far more efficient.
options: OS threads, because they provide true parallelism across all cores with kernel scheduling
Stackless coroutines, because I/O-bound work spends most time waiting and coroutines have orders of magnitude less memory overhead and faster context switching than threads
Green threads, because they balance the full call stack of threads with the lightweight scheduling of coroutines
Busy-waiting on a single thread, because I/O polling avoids context switch overhead entirely

questions.question: How does Go handle goroutine stack growth, and what is the initial stack size?
questions.explanation: Go goroutines start with a 4 KB initial stack. When a goroutine's stack exceeds this space (detected via stack bounds checks on function entry), the runtime allocates a new larger stack (typically 2× the current size) and copies the old stack frame-by-frame, adjusting interior pointers using adjustpointers(). This contiguous stack growth approach replaced the earlier split-stacks design in Go 1.3.
options: Go pre-allocates 8 MB per goroutine, matching the OS thread default, but uses copy-on-write to share pages
Goroutines start at 4 KB and grow by allocating a new larger stack and copying the old one, adjusting pointers to the new locations
Goroutines use segmented stacks (split stacks) where each segment is 4 KB, chained together as needed
Go allocates a fixed 64 KB stack per goroutine and panics if the stack overflows, requiring developers to manually size goroutine stacks

questions.question: What does Rust's Future::poll method return, and how does the Waker work?
questions.explanation: Future::poll returns either Poll::Ready(value) indicating the future has completed, or Poll::Pending indicating it's not done yet. When returning Pending, the future must have arranged for the Waker (from the Context parameter) to be called at some point in the future when progress can be made. The executor uses the Waker to know when to re-poll the future. This push-based model avoids wasted polling — the future only gets polled when something has changed.
options: Poll::Ready(result) immediately or Poll::Pending with a Waker callback that the future registers to be called when it can make progress, allowing the executor to re-poll only when needed
Poll::Finished or Poll::Waiting, with Waker being a thread that wakes up the polling thread when data is available
Poll::Ready or Poll::Error, with Waker acting as a channel that sends results between threads
Poll::Done or Poll::Retry, with Waker being a callback that automatically retries the future on failure
